You are given one minute to prepare your talk, and are given a pencil and paper to make notes.
The examiner asks you to talk for 1 to 2 minutes, stops you after 2 minutes, and asks one or two questions on the same topic.
Using the points on the task card effectively, and making notes during the preparation time, will help you think of appropriate things to say, structure your talk, and keep talking for 2 minutes.
Part 2 lasts 3–4 minutes, including the preparation time.
This part of the test focuses on the ability to speak at length on a given topic (without further prompts from the examiner), using appropriate language and organising ideas coherently.
You will need to draw on your own experience to complete the long turn.
You and the examiner discuss issues related to the topic in Part 2 in a more general and abstract way and, where appropriate, in greater depth.
The examiner asks you general questions on some familiar topics such as home, family, work, studies and interests.
Ability to express and justify opinions and to analyse, discuss and speculate about issues.
This criterion refers to the range of vocabulary used and the precision with which meanings and attitudes can be expressed.
grow / cultivate / nurture some plants / flowers / crops / greenery / the garden / a yard / a pot of plant / bensai
something that can be dangerous or cause damage
coffee beans, coffee mugs, coffee machine, ...
Your word choices need to be varied
The adequacy and appropriacy of the words used
I like coffee. I can't live without coffee.
I'm obsessed with coffee, to an extent that I can't live without it.
The ability to paraphrase (get round a vocabulary gap by using other words) with or without noticeable hesitation
This refers to the range and the accurate and appropriate use of the test takers' grammatical resource.
The length and complexity of the spoken sentences

I won the lottery and I bought an island.

as ... as someone thought it would be
The appropriate use of subordinate clauses
The range of sentence structures, especially to move elements around for information focus

because -> due to the fact that
Due to the fact that it's raining outside, it's very cold at the moment.
The number of grammatical errors in a given amount of speech
This refers to the ability to talk with normal levels of continuity, rate and effort and to link ideas and language together to form coherent, connected speech.

All in all / In a nutshell / Overall / I guess what I'm really trying to say is ...
The use of cohesive devices (e.g. connectors, pronouns and conjunctions) within and between sentences
This criterion refers to the ability to produce comprehensible speech to fulfil the Speaking test requirements.

attached.title: Where do I do it, and why?

plain.content: I train at the local community pool because it's a 50-metre pool so I have to swim a bit further to get to the other end. I choose this pool as it's generally quiet and I usually get a lane to myself.

realHTML.content: I train at the local community pool because it's a 50-metre pool so I have to swim a bit further to get to the other end. I choose this pool as it's generally quiet and I usually get a lane to myself.

attached.title: Is it good for me, and why?

plain.content: Swimming is a great sport as it helps your cardio-vascular fitness. It also builds muscle and helps with flexibility, so it's good for people who are older, like me.

realHTML.content: Swimming is a great sport as it helps your cardio-vascular fitness. It also builds muscle and helps with flexibility, so it's good for people who are older, like me.

attached.title: What equipment do I need, and why?

plain.content:
You don't need that much equipment unless you want to join a squad and train. Your basic requirements are a cap and goggles and of course,  a swimming suit. You need goggles as the pools in Australia have a lot of chlorine and it will sting your eyes. If you want to get into a squad and do some training, you will need a kick board and fins.

Synonym:
swimming suit - a pair of togs (Aussie slang)
do some training - get some serious training (verb replacement)

attached.title: Start at the beginning by commenting on the topic.

plain.content: Today, I'm going to talk about something that I own that is really important to me. It's my piano, an upright piano which was made in Germany. It's a beautiful colour, dark mahogany and is still in very good condition.

realHTML.content: Today, I'm going to talk about something that I own that is really important to me. It's my piano, an upright piano which was made in Germany. It's a beautiful colour, dark mahogany and is still in very good condition.

attached.title: Then move on to each bullet point

plain.content:
Start at the first bullet point and then keep going in a logical manner 
Even though I said the piano was made in Germany, I actually got it in Ireland. It was so long ago, I don't really remember the exact place where I bought it. It was probably in a popular music store in Dublin, where I lived at the time. It was a pretty major purchase as I had to save up for quite a few years to afford it. When I was younger, I was a music student at university, so I was keen to continue playing the piano, in order to have a nice pastime at home. 
As I mentioned, I have had it for a long time, possibly 33 years, or maybe more, a life-time! Even though I migrated from Ireland to Australia, I paid to have it shipped over, it was definitely worth the expense. The piano has been moved from house to house since then and always takes pride of place in my home. 
What do I use it for? Well, obviously to play music. I'm not as good on the piano as I used to be, because unfortunately I don't practice enough. As they say, practice makes perfect, and I am definitely not perfect. I really need to play it more often, but life gets in the way. 
It is very important to me because it's something I have had for my entire adult life. I was very proud that I had saved enough money to buy it, so I really wanted to make sure I kept it. My children have learned how to play the piano, so it has been well used. Hopefully, some day, I will teach my grandchildren how to play it as well.

summary.title: Actually,I haven't been lost in a long time, but seeing this question, I suddenly remembered my freshman year, just entering college and having an experience of getting lost.Since I just entered the school, I am not familiar with the environment. After attending an activity with my roommate, I can't remember the way back to my dorm.We looked at each other and laughed. After that we turned on the phone navigation to find back.

attached.title: Actually, I haven't been lost for a lonngggg time, but upon seeing this question, I immediately thought of an incident that happened during the freshman year in my uni days.

In terms of what happened, I vaguely remember that I had just got enrolled in my college back then, and it was right after attending an activity, maybe a lecture, with my roommate. We just simply couldn't find the way that we came from.

Well of course my initial thought was this is so embarrassing. But given the fact that I only just started my uni life, it wasn't long till I persuaded myself that it's more important to familiarise with my surroundings than afflicting self-blame, obviously. That was when I noticed my roommate was as dumbfounded as I was. So we just looked at each other and laughed.

Eventually, I know it might sound a bit silly but we actually got our phones out, got on a navigation app and followed the little blue triangle back to our dorm. Gotta say I found this little adventure that I shared with my roomie quite exhilarating!

The first thing that I could remember was
The second thing I'd like to mention is, ...
... is totally my cup of tea
The heart of the matter is…
I cannot speak to ____, but I can say…
While ____ is important, it’s also important to remember that…
Before we leave this subject, I’d like to add…
Well it really depends on how you define ...
I would say it actually all comes to the definition of the word ""
a type of food that is sweet
something (that) people eat after main course
e.g. iPad - it's like a television, but way smaller.
No clue what the topic is about?
Explain you've got no idea what the activity is like
How to get familiar with a wider variety of topics quickly?

Listening to yourself can be strange at first but you get used to it. Listen to a recording of a fluent English speaker (a short audio file) and then record yourself repeating what they said. Compare the difference and try again. Humans are natural mimics so you will find yourself getting better and better. Soundcloud is an excellent tool for voice recording as you or your teacher can make notes about your errors.

Reading aloud is not the same as speaking naturally. However, it is very useful for exercising the vocal muscles. Practise for 5 or 10 minutes a day and you will begin to notice which sounds are difficult for you to produce. Find transcripts of natural dialogues, such as these here, and practise acting them with a friend, you will also learn common phrases which we use when speaking.
Sing along to English songs while you’re driving or in the shower.

The lyrics to pop songs are often conversational so you can learn lots of common expressions by listening to them. Humans are also able to remember words when used together with music which is why it is difficult to remember poems but easy to remember the words to songs. Here are some songs to get started with.
Watch short video clips and pause and repeat what you hear.

YouTube is an amazing resource for language learners and you probably already have your favourite clips. My advice is to watch short clips and really study them. With longer videos, you may find your attention wanders. The key to improving by watching videos is to really listen carefully and use the pause button to focus on sounds and words. Many YouTube videos now have captions.
Learn vowel and consonant sounds in English.

The Phonemic chart is a list of the different vowel and consonant sounds in English. Learning how to make these sounds and then using them to pronounce words correctly will really help you speak English clearly. This is a great resource from the British Council.
What is schwa you might be asking? Well, it’s the most common sound in English. We use it all the time in words like ‘teacher’ and ‘around’.
Learn about weak and strong forms of common words.

When you know about the ‘schwa’ sound, you will listen to native speakers in a different way. English is a stress-timed language which means that we use a combination of strong and weak forms of some words. For example, which words do we stress in the following sentence?
I want to go for a drink tonight.
How do native speakers pronounce to / for / a in the sentence? We use the schwa sound so it sounds like:
I wanna go ferra drink tenigh.
Learn how and when to use weak forms and your speaking will improve overnight.
You will also learn to focus on stressed words when listening to fast, native-speaker English and you will finally be able to understand us!

When words have more than one syllable, we stress one or more of them. For example, the word intelligent has four syllables but which syllable do we stress? Remember that the small vertical mark above the word identifies the stressed syllable: /ɪnˈtel.ɪ.dʒənt/

Sentence stress refers to the word or words we stress in a phrase of a sentence. When we stress a word, we help the listener understand what is important. If we stress the wrong word or don’t stress the key word, the listener may get confused or not realise what is important in the sentence.
Identify fixed and semi-fixed phrases and practise them.
Fixed phrases usually contain between 3 and 7 words and include items like:

A conversation is made of grammatical structures, vocabulary and fixed or semi-fixed phrases. In fact, to tell the truth , on the whole, most of the time, my friends and I , communicate with each other in a series of fixed and semi-fixed expressions.
Learn the communicative function of these phrases and practise how to pronounce them (remember weak forms, which words are stressed) and use them in your everyday conversation.
Words don’t like being alone. They prefer to hang out with their friends and, just like people, some words form close friendships and other never speak to each other.

Yellow doesn’t get on well with hair. Maybe yellow is jealous of blond because blond and hair are frequently seen out together having a great time. Yellow doesn’t understand why hair prefers blond because yellow and blond are so similar.
Listen carefully for common combinations of words.

Short and small have similar meanings but people have short hair not small hair. High and tall are often not so different but people have high hopes but not tall hopes. Foxes are sly not devious. Hours can be happy but are never cheerful. Idiots are stupid but rarely silly.
Replace regular verbs with phrasal verbs.
Many learners of English don’t understand why native speakers use so many phrasal verbs where there are normal verbs (usually with Latin roots) which have the same meaning.
English was originally a Germanic language which imported lots of Latin vocabulary after the Norman conquest in the 11th century.
Regardless of the historical factors, the fact is that native English speakers use lots and lots of phrasal verbs.

If you want to understand us, then try to include them in your conversation. If you make a mistake, you’ll probably make us laugh but you are unlikely to confuse us as we can usually guess what you want to say from the context. Phrasal verbs are spatial and originally referred to movement so when you learn a new one, make physical movements while saying them to help you remember.

Many of our responses are automatic (Right, OK, no problem, alright, fine thanks, just a minute, you’re welcome, fine by me, let’s do it!, yup, no way! you’re joking, right?, Do I have to? etc.) Collect these short automatic responses and start using them.
Practise telling stories and using narrative tenses.

Humans are designed to tell stories. We use the past simple, past continuous and past perfect for telling stories but when the listener is hooked (very interested), they feel like they are actually experiencing the story right now. So, we often use present tenses to make our stories more dramatic!
Learn when to pause for effect.

Speaking quickly in English does not make you an effective English speaker. Knowing when to pause to give the listener time to think about what you have said, respond appropriately, and predict what you are going to say does. Imagine you’re an actor on a stage, pausing keeps people interested. Great strategy if you need to speak English in public.
Learn about typical pronunciation problems in your first language.

Japanese learners find it difficult to identify and produce ‘r‘ and ‘l‘ sounds; Spanish don’t distinguish between ‘b‘ and ‘v‘; Germans often use a ‘v‘ sound when they should use a ‘w‘. Find out about the problems people who speak your first language have when speaking English and you will know what you need to focus on.

Chunking means joining words together to make meaningful units. You don’t need to analyse every word to use a phrase. Look at the phrase: Nice to meet you. It’s a short phrase (4 words) which can be remembered as a single item. It is also an example of ellipsis (leaving words out) because the words ‘It’ and ‘is’ are missing at the beginning of the phrase. However, we don’t need to include them. Learn more here.
Choose an accent you like and imitate it.

We often have an emotional connection with certain nationalities. Do you have more of an interest in British culture or American culture? Do you support Manchester United or Arsenal? Deciding what variety of English you want to learn is your first step.
Find an actor/actress you like and identify what makes them powerful speakers.

Do you want to sound like Barack Obama, Benedict Cumberbatch (Sherlock Homes) Beyonce or Steve Jobs? If you want to sound like David Beckham, I advise you to reconsider, unless you want to sound like a young girl!
Use a mirror and / or a sheet of paper for identifying aspirated and non-aspirated sounds.
Aspirated sounds are those with a short burst of here, such as ‘p‘ in ‘pen, and unaspirated sounds have no or little air, such as the ‘b‘ in ‘Ben‘. Watch this video to learn more.

Tongue twisters are phrases designed to improve your pronunciation of particular sounds. Here is a list for kids but it’s great fun. Have a go now.Try saying this phrase quickly: What a terrible tongue twister. What a terrible tongue twister. What a terrible tongue twister.
Practise spelling names, numbers and dates aloud.
This may seem very basic to some of you but if you don’t practise, you forget how to say them.Have a go here at numbers here and at place names here.

Intonation (when the pitch of the voice goes up and down) is complex in English but it is very important as it expresses the feeling or emotion of the speaker. Here is an amusing introduction to intonation.

The articulators are the parts of the mouth we use to turn sound into speech. They can be fixed parts (the teeth, behind the teeth and the roof of the mouth) and mobile parts (the tongue, the lips, the soft palate, and the jaw).

After looking at places of articulation, practise making the movements that native speakers use when they speak. Here’s a video and remember to open the jaws, move the lips and get your tongue moving!
Learn why English is a stress-timed language.

The rhythm of the language is based on stressed syllables so we shorten the unstressed syllables to fit the rhythm. Syllable-timed languages (such as Spanish) take the same time to pronounce each syllable. Here’s an explanation which might explain why you speak English like a robot or watch this funny clip here.
Learn about ellipsis, assimilation and linking sounds.
Learn how to interrupt and interject politely and successfully.
Studies show that you command attention and demonstrate authority with a deeper vocal tone, especially men. This is particularly important if you have to speak in public. Here is a quick guide.
Listen and read along to poetry (or rap songs) to practise the rhythm of English.
Limericks (short, funny, rhyming poems) are really useful and demonstrate how English is stress-timed and how we use weak forms.
Watch this video or study this list of 100 common exclamations here.
Paraphrasing is when we repeat what we have just said to make it clear to the listener or when we repeat what the other person has said by using different words. Here are a few to get started.
Contractions make your speech more efficient because they save time and energy. Say ‘should not’ and then say ‘shouldn’t’: which is easier to say? Very common in fluent speech.
A state of quiet satisfaction and ease.
"After a long day's work in the garden, he sat on the porch with a deep sense of contentment."
A feeling of happy satisfaction and enjoyment.
"She was genuinely pleased to hear that her old friend was visiting from overseas."
A strong feeling of great pleasure and happiness.
"The surprise reunion was a joyful occasion that brought tears to everyone's eyes."
A feeling of immense happiness and exhilaration.
"When she saw her name at the top of the list, she felt a wave of pure elation."
An overwhelming feeling of great happiness or joyful excitement.
"The fans were ecstatic when their team scored the winning goal in the final seconds."
Losing my mind (in a good way)
Sadness from the non-fulfillment of one's hopes.
"He was bitterly disappointed when the much-anticipated event was cancelled."
A general feeling of being unhappy.
"A touch of sadness crossed her face as she packed away the old photographs."
Deep sorrow, especially that caused by someone's death.
"The entire community was grief-stricken after the tragic accident."
going through a lot right now
A feeling of deep distress caused by loss or misfortune, normally used for formal situations.
"He expressed his deep sorrow for their loss with a heartfelt letter."
Severe mental or physical pain or suffering.
"She let out an anguished cry upon hearing the terrible news from the hospital."
A feeling of slight anger or irritation.
"She was clearly annoyed by the constant buzzing of the fly in the room."
The feeling of being upset by an inability to change or achieve something.
"After an hour of trying to assemble the furniture, he threw the instructions down in frustration."
I really can't deal with this.
Ugh, I'm so done with it.
I'm so fed up with his bullshit.
- **Definition:** A strong feeling of annoyance, displeasure, or hostility.
- **Collocations:** A flash of anger, simmering anger, blind anger, get angry with.
- **Example:** "A flash of anger appeared in his eyes before he took a deep breath to calm himself."
* **How you might say it:** So mad; pissed off; ropeable; seeing red.

You don't need that much equipment unless you want to join a squad and train. Your basic requirements are a cap and goggles and of course,  a swimming suit. swimming suit - a pair of togs (Aussie slang)
do some training - get some serious training (verb replacement)

attached.title: Start at the beginning by commenting on the topic.

plain.content: Today, I'm going to talk about something that I own that is really important to me. It's my piano, an upright piano which was made in Germany. It's a beautiful colour, dark mahogany and is still in very good condition.

realHTML.content: Today, I'm going to talk about something that I own that is really important to me. It's my piano, an upright piano which was made in Germany. It's a beautiful colour, dark mahogany and is still in very good condition.

attached.title: Then move on to each bullet point

plain.content:
Start at the first bullet point and then keep going in a logical manner 
Even though I said the piano was made in Germany, I actually got it in Ireland. It was so long ago, I don't really remember the exact place where I bought it. It was probably in a popular music store in Dublin, where I lived at the time. It was a pretty major purchase as I had to save up for quite a few years to afford it. When I was younger, I was a music student at university, so I was keen to continue playing the piano, in order to have a nice pastime at home. 
As I mentioned, I have had it for a long time, possibly 33 years, or maybe more, a life-time! Even though I migrated from Ireland to Australia, I paid to have it shipped over, it was definitely worth the expense. The piano has been moved from house to house since then and always takes pride of place in my home. 
What do I use it for? Well, obviously to play music. I'm not as good on the piano as I used to be, because unfortunately I don't practice enough. As they say, practice makes perfect, and I am definitely not perfect. I really need to play it more often, but life gets in the way. 
It is very important to me because it's something I have had for my entire adult life. I was very proud that I had saved enough money to buy it, so I really wanted to make sure I kept it. My children have learned how to play the piano, so it has been well used. Hopefully, some day, I will teach my grandchildren how to play it as well.

attached.title: Actually, I haven't been lost for a lonngggg time, but upon seeing this question, I immediately thought of an incident that happened during the freshman year in my uni days.

In terms of what happened, I vaguely remember that I had just got enrolled in my college back then, and it was right after attending an activity, maybe a lecture, with my roommate. We just simply couldn't find the way that we came from.

Well of course my initial thought was this is so embarrassing. But given the fact that I only just started my uni life, it wasn't long till I persuaded myself that it's more important to familiarise with my surroundings than afflicting self-blame, obviously. That was when I noticed my roommate was as dumbfounded as I was. So we just looked at each other and laughed.

Eventually, I know it might sound a bit silly but we actually got our phones out, got on a navigation app and followed the little blue triangle back to our dorm. Gotta say I found this little adventure that I shared with my roomie quite exhilarating!
Look at the questions at different sides

The first thing that happened was
The first thing that I could remember was
The second thing I'd like to mention is, ...
... is totally my cup of tea
The heart of the matter is…
I cannot speak to ____, but I can say…
While ____ is important, it’s also important to remember that…
Before we leave this subject, I’d like to add…
Well it really depends on how you define ...
I would say it actually all comes to the definition of the word ""
a type of food that is sweet
something (that) people eat after main course
e.g. iPad - it's like a television, but way smaller.
No clue what the topic is about?
Explain you've got no idea what the activity is like
How to get familiar with a wider variety of topics quickly?

Listening to yourself can be strange at first but you get used to it. Listen to a recording of a fluent English speaker (a short audio file) and then record yourself repeating what they said. Compare the difference and try again. Humans are natural mimics so you will find yourself getting better and better. Soundcloud is an excellent tool for voice recording as you or your teacher can make notes about your errors.

Reading aloud is not the same as speaking naturally. However, it is very useful for exercising the vocal muscles. Practise for 5 or 10 minutes a day and you will begin to notice which sounds are difficult for you to produce. Find transcripts of natural dialogues, such as these here, and practise acting them with a friend, you will also learn common phrases which we use when speaking.
Sing along to English songs while you’re driving or in the shower.

The lyrics to pop songs are often conversational so you can learn lots of common expressions by listening to them. Humans are also able to remember words when used together with music which is why it is difficult to remember poems but easy to remember the words to songs. Here are some songs to get started with.
Watch short video clips and pause and repeat what you hear.

YouTube is an amazing resource for language learners and you probably already have your favourite clips. My advice is to watch short clips and really study them. With longer videos, you may find your attention wanders. The key to improving by watching videos is to really listen carefully and use the pause button to focus on sounds and words. Many YouTube videos now have captions.
Learn vowel and consonant sounds in English.

The Phonemic chart is a list of the different vowel and consonant sounds in English. Learning how to make these sounds and then using them to pronounce words correctly will really help you speak English clearly. This is a great resource from the British Council.
What is schwa you might be asking? Well, it’s the most common sound in English. We use it all the time in words like ‘teacher’ and ‘around’.
Learn about weak and strong forms of common words.

When you know about the ‘schwa’ sound, you will listen to native speakers in a different way. English is a stress-timed language which means that we use a combination of strong and weak forms of some words. For example, which words do we stress in the following sentence?
I want to go for a drink tonight.
How do native speakers pronounce to / for / a in the sentence? We use the schwa sound so it sounds like:
I wanna go ferra drink tenigh.
Learn how and when to use weak forms and your speaking will improve overnight.
You will also learn to focus on stressed words when listening to fast, native-speaker English and you will finally be able to understand us!

When words have more than one syllable, we stress one or more of them. For example, the word intelligent has four syllables but which syllable do we stress? Remember that the small vertical mark above the word identifies the stressed syllable: /ɪnˈtel.ɪ.dʒənt/

Sentence stress refers to the word or words we stress in a phrase of a sentence. When we stress a word, we help the listener understand what is important. If we stress the wrong word or don’t stress the key word, the listener may get confused or not realise what is important in the sentence.
Identify fixed and semi-fixed phrases and practise them.
Fixed phrases usually contain between 3 and 7 words and include items like:

A conversation is made of grammatical structures, vocabulary and fixed or semi-fixed phrases. In fact, to tell the truth , on the whole, most of the time, my friends and I , communicate with each other in a series of fixed and semi-fixed expressions.
Learn the communicative function of these phrases and practise how to pronounce them (remember weak forms, which words are stressed) and use them in your everyday conversation.
Words don’t like being alone. They prefer to hang out with their friends and, just like people, some words form close friendships and other never speak to each other.

Yellow doesn’t get on well with hair. Maybe yellow is jealous of blond because blond and hair are frequently seen out together having a great time. Yellow doesn’t understand why hair prefers blond because yellow and blond are so similar.
Listen carefully for common combinations of words.

Short and small have similar meanings but people have short hair not small hair. High and tall are often not so different but people have high hopes but not tall hopes. Foxes are sly not devious. Hours can be happy but are never cheerful. Idiots are stupid but rarely silly.
Replace regular verbs with phrasal verbs.
Many learners of English don’t understand why native speakers use so many phrasal verbs where there are normal verbs (usually with Latin roots) which have the same meaning.
English was originally a Germanic language which imported lots of Latin vocabulary after the Norman conquest in the 11th century.
Regardless of the historical factors, the fact is that native English speakers use lots and lots of phrasal verbs.

If you want to understand us, then try to include them in your conversation. If you make a mistake, you’ll probably make us laugh but you are unlikely to confuse us as we can usually guess what you want to say from the context. Phrasal verbs are spatial and originally referred to movement so when you learn a new one, make physical movements while saying them to help you remember.

Many of our responses are automatic (Right, OK, no problem, alright, fine thanks, just a minute, you’re welcome, fine by me, let’s do it!, yup, no way! you’re joking, right?, Do I have to? etc.) Collect these short automatic responses and start using them.
Practise telling stories and using narrative tenses.

Humans are designed to tell stories. We use the past simple, past continuous and past perfect for telling stories but when the listener is hooked (very interested), they feel like they are actually experiencing the story right now. So, we often use present tenses to make our stories more dramatic!
Learn when to pause for effect.

Speaking quickly in English does not make you an effective English speaker. Knowing when to pause to give the listener time to think about what you have said, respond appropriately, and predict what you are going to say does. Imagine you’re an actor on a stage, pausing keeps people interested. Great strategy if you need to speak English in public.
Learn about typical pronunciation problems in your first language.

Japanese learners find it difficult to identify and produce ‘r‘ and ‘l‘ sounds; Spanish don’t distinguish between ‘b‘ and ‘v‘; Germans often use a ‘v‘ sound when they should use a ‘w‘. Find out about the problems people who speak your first language have when speaking English and you will know what you need to focus on.

Chunking means joining words together to make meaningful units. You don’t need to analyse every word to use a phrase. Look at the phrase: Nice to meet you. It’s a short phrase (4 words) which can be remembered as a single item. It is also an example of ellipsis (leaving words out) because the words ‘It’ and ‘is’ are missing at the beginning of the phrase. However, we don’t need to include them. Learn more here.
Choose an accent you like and imitate it.

We often have an emotional connection with certain nationalities. Do you have more of an interest in British culture or American culture? Do you support Manchester United or Arsenal? Deciding what variety of English you want to learn is your first step.
Find an actor/actress you like and identify what makes them powerful speakers.

Do you want to sound like Barack Obama, Benedict Cumberbatch (Sherlock Homes) Beyonce or Steve Jobs? If you want to sound like David Beckham, I advise you to reconsider, unless you want to sound like a young girl!
Use a mirror and / or a sheet of paper for identifying aspirated and non-aspirated sounds.
Aspirated sounds are those with a short burst of here, such as ‘p‘ in ‘pen, and unaspirated sounds have no or little air, such as the ‘b‘ in ‘Ben‘. Watch this video to learn more.

Tongue twisters are phrases designed to improve your pronunciation of particular sounds. Here is a list for kids but it’s great fun. Have a go now.Try saying this phrase quickly: What a terrible tongue twister. What a terrible tongue twister. What a terrible tongue twister.
Practise spelling names, numbers and dates aloud.
This may seem very basic to some of you but if you don’t practise, you forget how to say them.Have a go here at numbers here and at place names here.

Intonation (when the pitch of the voice goes up and down) is complex in English but it is very important as it expresses the feeling or emotion of the speaker. Here is an amusing introduction to intonation.

The articulators are the parts of the mouth we use to turn sound into speech. They can be fixed parts (the teeth, behind the teeth and the roof of the mouth) and mobile parts (the tongue, the lips, the soft palate, and the jaw).

After looking at places of articulation, practise making the movements that native speakers use when they speak. Here’s a video and remember to open the jaws, move the lips and get your tongue moving!
Learn why English is a stress-timed language.

The rhythm of the language is based on stressed syllables so we shorten the unstressed syllables to fit the rhythm. Syllable-timed languages (such as Spanish) take the same time to pronounce each syllable. Here’s an explanation which might explain why you speak English like a robot or watch this funny clip here.
Learn about ellipsis, assimilation and linking sounds.
Learn how to interrupt and interject politely and successfully.
Studies show that you command attention and demonstrate authority with a deeper vocal tone, especially men. This is particularly important if you have to speak in public. Here is a quick guide.
Listen and read along to poetry (or rap songs) to practise the rhythm of English.
Limericks (short, funny, rhyming poems) are really useful and demonstrate how English is stress-timed and how we use weak forms.
Watch this video or study this list of 100 common exclamations here.
Paraphrasing is when we repeat what we have just said to make it clear to the listener or when we repeat what the other person has said by using different words. Here are a few to get started.
Contractions make your speech more efficient because they save time and energy. Say ‘should not’ and then say ‘shouldn’t’: which is easier to say? Very common in fluent speech.
A state of quiet satisfaction and ease.
"After a long day's work in the garden, he sat on the porch with a deep sense of contentment."
A feeling of happy satisfaction and enjoyment.
"She was genuinely pleased to hear that her old friend was visiting from overseas."
A strong feeling of great pleasure and happiness.
"The surprise reunion was a joyful occasion that brought tears to everyone's eyes."
A feeling of immense happiness and exhilaration.
"When she saw her name at the top of the list, she felt a wave of pure elation."
An overwhelming feeling of great happiness or joyful excitement.
"The fans were ecstatic when their team scored the winning goal in the final seconds."
Losing my mind (in a good way)
Sadness from the non-fulfillment of one's hopes.
"He was bitterly disappointed when the much-anticipated event was cancelled."
A general feeling of being unhappy.
"A touch of sadness crossed her face as she packed away the old photographs."
Deep sorrow, especially that caused by someone's death.
"The entire community was grief-stricken after the tragic accident."
going through a lot right now
A feeling of deep distress caused by loss or misfortune, normally used for formal situations.
"He expressed his deep sorrow for their loss with a heartfelt letter."
Severe mental or physical pain or suffering.
"She let out an anguished cry upon hearing the terrible news from the hospital."
A feeling of slight anger or irritation.
"She was clearly annoyed by the constant buzzing of the fly in the room."
The feeling of being upset by an inability to change or achieve something.
"After an hour of trying to assemble the furniture, he threw the instructions down in frustration."
I really can't deal with this.
Ugh, I'm so done with it.
I'm so fed up with his bullshit.
- **Definition:** A strong feeling of annoyance, displeasure, or hostility.
- **Collocations:** A flash of anger, simmering anger, blind anger, get angry with.
- **Example:** "A flash of anger appeared in his eyes before he took a deep breath to calm himself."
* **How you might say it:** So mad; pissed off; ropeable; seeing red.
- **Collocations:** A fit of rage, fly into a rage, filled with rage, absolutely enraged.
- **Example:** "He flew into a rage when he discovered his new car had been badly scratched."
* **How you might say it:** Absolutely lost it; went ballistic; flipped out; hit the roof.
- **Collocations:** Unleash fury, righteous fury, incandescent with fury, absolutely furious.
- **Example:** "She was absolutely furious about the injustice of the court's decision."
* **How you might say it:** Absolutely livid; fuming; beside myself with anger.
- **Definition:** Anxiety that something bad or unpleasant will happen.
- **Collocations:** A sense of apprehension, filled with apprehension, feel apprehensive about.
- **Example:** "He felt apprehensive about walking home alone after dark."
* **How you might say it:** A bit nervous; feeling a bit sketchy about it; on edge; having a bad feeling.
- **Definition:** A feeling of worry, nervousness, or unease.
- **Collocations:** High anxiety, suffer from anxiety, crippling anxiety, feel anxious about.
- **Example:** "It's normal to feel anxious before a major presentation."
* **How you might say it:** Stressing out; freaking out; my anxiety is through the roof.
- **Definition:** An unpleasant emotion caused by a perceived threat.
- **Collocations:** Gripped by fear, overcome a fear, constant fear, deathly afraid of.
- **Example:** "Even as an adult, he was still deathly afraid of public speaking."
* **How you might say it:** Properly scared; bricking it; scared the crap out of me; gives me the creeps/cringe. cringey.
- **Definition:** An intense feeling of fear, shock, and disgust.
- **Collocations:** A gasp of horror, a look of horror, sheer horror, absolutely horrified.
- **Example:** "She watched in absolute horror as the two cars collided on the highway."
* **How you might say it:** That's so messed up; completely grossed out; my jaw dropped.
- **Collocations:** A reign of terror, scream in terror, frozen with terror, absolutely terrified.
- **Example:** "The loud, unexplained crash from downstairs left him frozen with terror."
* **How you might say it:** Petrified; scared the life out of me; nearly had a heart attack.
- **Definition:** Mild astonishment caused by something unexpected.
- **Collocations:** Take by surprise, a pleasant surprise, to my surprise, genuinely surprised.
- **Example:** "To my surprise, the boss announced a public holiday for Friday."
* **How you might say it:** No way!; For real?; Didn't see that coming; caught me off guard.
- **Definition:** A feeling of great surprise or wonder.
- **Collocations:** In amazement, to my amazement, sheer amazement, completely amazed.
- **Example:** "He stared in sheer amazement at the incredible Northern Lights."
* **How you might say it:** That's insane; mind blown 🤯; that's wild; holy crap.
- **Collocations:** Utter astonishment, a gasp of astonishment, look of astonishment, completely astonished.
- **Example:** "She looked at him in utter astonishment, unable to believe he had completed the marathon."
* **How you might say it:** Shook; gobsmacked; I can't even; speechless.
- **Definition:** A feeling of reverential respect mixed with fear or wonder.
- **Collocations:** In awe of, inspire awe, a sense of awe, completely awestruck.
- **Example:** "Standing at the base of the ancient pyramids, they were completely awestruck."
* **How you might say it:** That's next level; so epic; unreal; beyond words.
- **Definition:** A sudden upsetting or surprising event or experience.
- **Collocations:** A state of shock, absorb the shock, a terrible shock, visibly shocked.
- **Example:** "The community was in a state of shock following the news of the factory's closure."
* **How you might say it:** Wait, what?!; jaw was on the floor; couldn't believe it; shook to my core.
- **Definition:** A feeling of liking or affection for someone.
- **Collocations:** A great fondness for, develop a fondness, grow fond of.
- **Example:** "He had grown fond of the stray cat that visited his porch every day."
* **How you might say it:** Got a soft spot for; really rate them; kinda into it.
- **Definition:** A gentle feeling of fondness or liking.
- **Collocations:** Show affection, a display of affection, genuine affection, an affectionate gesture.
- **Example:** "A dog wagging its tail is a clear display of affection."
* **How you might say it:** Super lovely to someone; being all cute; giving good vibes.
- **Definition:** An intense feeling of deep affection.
- **Collocations:** Fall in love, true love, unconditional love, a loving family.
- **Example:** "They have built a loving home based on years of trust and mutual respect."
* **How you might say it:** Totally fallen for someone; head over heels; my person; so in love.
- **Collocations:** Pure adoration, a look of adoration, mutual adoration, an adoring fan.
- **Example:** "The new parents gazed at their sleeping baby with a look of pure adoration."
* **How you might say it:** Absolutely worships them; thinks the sun shines out of them; completely obsessed with.
- **Definition:** Love, loyalty, or enthusiasm for a person.
- **Collocations:** Blind devotion, lifelong devotion, show devotion to, a devoted partner.
- **Example:** "Her devotion to animal welfare led her to open a rescue shelter."
* **How you might say it:** Would do anything for them; always has their back; super loyal; ride or die.
